coredata.dc:title: Enhanced capacitive performance by synergistic effect of interfacial functional groups and doping defects in honeycomb-like multi-chamber carbon
coredata.dc:creator: Zheng, Yujing
Lian, Yue
Guo, Haixian
Zhao, Jing
Zhang, Huaihao
coredata.dc:description: As a typical double-layer capacitive electrode material, carbon nanomaterials show desirable application potential in the field of capacitive deionization (CDI) and supercapacitors (SCs). In this work, interfacial functional groups and B\O co-doping defects are simultaneously introduced into multi-chamber carbon with confinement effect, and their practical applications in CDI and flexible capacitors are also studied. Specifically, the oxygen-rich carbon source and boron source, dissolved and mixed completely via co-crystallization method, released amount of gas during the pyrolysis process to produce multi-chamber honeycomb-like carbon with ultrathin walls, which were further introduced into abundant interfacial functional groups by dopamine to optimize the carbon surface. At the same time, part of boron atoms entered the carbon lattice to form boron doping at high temperature, followed by the integration with the oxygen heteroatoms in carbon source to generate abundant boron and oxygen codoping defects. The as-prepared diatomic defects by the synergistic effect promote the wettability and pseudocapacitance of the material. In addition, the derived carbon materials are endowed with good hydrophilic ability and surface activity under the modification of dopamine functional groups. The ideal hydrophilic ability ensures that the porous structure of material can be infiltrated by electrolyte to provide large effective specific surface area, which enhances the electrical double-layered capacity. The interfacial functional groups also offer additional pseudocapacitance, so as to improve the ion adsorption capacity of PDA-BOC.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon
Dopamine
Capacitive deionization
Supercapacitors
Defect

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Key Research & Development Program ( 2021YFC2102205 ), Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (grant number KYCX21_3198 ), the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD), and Top-notch Academic Programs Project of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (TAPP, PPZY2015B112 ). The related measure and analysis instrument for this work was supported by the Testing Center of Yangzhou University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of CDI application: Batch mode (A) and single-pass mode CDI (B).
Schematic illustration of the preparation process (A), XRD patterns (B), Raman spectra (C) SEM images (D, E) and TEM images (F, G) of PDA-BOC.
XPS of PDA-BOC: survey spectrum (A); C 1 s (B); O 1 s (D); B 1 s (E); Structure (C) and TGA (F).

CV curves of BOC and PDA-BOC (A, B); GCD curves of PDA-BOC (C); Capacity of BOC and PDA-BOC (D); cycles performances (E); b value under cathode scan (the inset shows the current density plotted against scan rate at different potentials, F); CV curve with separation between total current (solid line) and capacitive current (shaded area) at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1 (G) and capacitance contribution of surface- and diffusion-controlled at different scan rates (H).

CDI performance tests: (A) Desalination performance of PDA-BOC electrodes in a 500 mg/L NaCl solution; (B) Ragone plots of ASAR vs SAC for PDA-BOC at different cell voltages; (C) Curves of desalination amount vs charging time of PDA-BOC and BOC; (D) Plots of conductivity and (E) The regeneration performance of the PDA-BOC electrodes at a cell voltage of 1.2 V with a flow rate of 32 mL min−1; (F) Plot of charge efficiency and electrosorption capacity vs cell voltage for PDA-BOC electrode.
Reduction rate of metal ion concentration in different water bodies: Yangtze River (A), Grand Canal (B), Slender West River (C) and Rain water (D).
Structure diagram of Flexible supercapacitor (A); Bending test (B); CV curve (C), GCD curves (D), capacity (E), Ragone (F) and cycles test (H) of PDA-BOC//PDA-BOC.
Salt adsorption performance comparison of CDI electrode materials.

head.ce:title: Enhanced capacitive performance by synergistic effect of interfacial functional groups and doping defects in honeycomb-like multi-chamber carbon

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou 225002, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou 225002, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a typical double-layer capacitive electrode material, carbon nanomaterials show desirable application potential in the field of capacitive deionization (CDI) and supercapacitors (SCs). In this work, interfacial functional groups and B\O co-doping defects are simultaneously introduced into multi-chamber carbon with confinement effect, and their practical applications in CDI and flexible capacitors are also studied. Specifically, the oxygen-rich carbon source and boron source, dissolved and mixed completely via co-crystallization method, released amount of gas during the pyrolysis process to produce multi-chamber honeycomb-like carbon with ultrathin walls, which were further introduced into abundant interfacial functional groups by dopamine to optimize the carbon surface. At the same time, part of boron atoms entered the carbon lattice to form boron doping at high temperature, followed by the integration with the oxygen heteroatoms in carbon source to generate abundant boron and oxygen codoping defects. The as-prepared diatomic defects by the synergistic effect promote the wettability and pseudocapacitance of the material. In addition, the derived carbon materials are endowed with good hydrophilic ability and surface activity under the modification of dopamine functional groups. The ideal hydrophilic ability ensures that the porous structure of material can be infiltrated by electrolyte to provide large effective specific surface area, which enhances the electrical double-layered capacity. The interfacial functional groups also offer additional pseudocapacitance, so as to improve the ion adsorption capacity of PDA-BOC.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The serious shortage of energy and freshwater has become a constant threat to current social development [1–3]. In this context, a soaring demand for energy storage/conversion and desalination technologies has been grown gradually. SCs and CDI have been extensively studied to tackle the crisis [4–10]. Particularly, SCs are a novel class of energy storage devices, which present two energy storage mode: Electric double-layer capacitors (EDLCs) and pseudocapacitors [11,12]. In EDLCs, the capacitance is acquired by highly reversible adsorption of ions at the electrolyte–electrode interface, bringing about the benefits of fast charging-discharging rate, high power density and environmentally friendliness [13,14]. Meanwhile, as a new electrochemical desalination technology developed from EDLC theory, CDI inherits the aforesaid superiorities as well [15]. Briefly, they are all composed of two electrodes, electrolyte and applied electric field. While their application aim is different: EDLCs manage to store electrical energy by adsorbing ions in a closed system, while the charge separation in the flowing brackish water results in the capacitance between two electrodes, conductive to salt removal in CDI. In short, how to improve the capacitive adsorption capacity of electrode materials is the common optimization direction in both devices [16–19].Fig. 1 .

Well known, the carbon materials show typically capacitive properties with great application potential [20,21]. Their design and optimization are the focus of current research, including morphological structure improvement, lattice defects and interfacial modification [22–24]. The key to morphology level design mainly depends on enlarging the effective specific surface area and structural stability (toughness grade), thus to achieve high specific capacitance directly. With regard to capacitance limit of pure carbon material itself, the lattice defects such as doping vacancies are added to the carbon lattice to enhance their electrical activity [25,26]. Interfacial modification is to graft or adjust functional groups on carbon materials surface in order to upgrade the interface properties [27,28]. All of them provide rich pseudocapacitance based on the electric double-layer behavior of carbon materials. In addition, the simplification and greening of carbon material preparation strategies is also one of the key points of carbon material preparation.

Here, boron and oxygen codoped three-dimensional multi-chamber carbon rich in COOH/C = O functional groups were synthesized by low-cost organic reagents. Meanwhile, dopamine was applied to optimize the functional group properties of the carbon surface, which greatly enhances the infiltration rate and effective accessible specific surface area of the electrolyte [29–32]. The merits of the material can be explained from morphology, lattice and interfacial modification: (1) Morphology: The honeycomb-like carbon in form of carbon sheets possesses a stable 3D multi-chamber configuration with high specific surface area, offering a solid foundation to the good EDLCs performance. (2) Lattice: Oxygen and boron doping defects from carbon sources synergistically reinforce the conductivity and pseudocapacitance. The doping B atom (smaller than C atom) serving as the active sites can contribute desirable reaction kinetics. Amorphous carbon materials with structural defects make progress in the edge effect, electrode interface and electrochemical performance. (3) Interfacial modification: The interface modification by dopamine further modifies the conductivity and wettability of the material, strengthens the ion interface capture ability and increases the capacitance of the material.

Synthesis of BOC: Sodium citrate (C6H5Na3O7·2H2O, ≥99%) and boric acid (H3BO3, AR) were dissolved in DI water at a mass ratio of 5:1. The dissolved solution was kept at 60 ℃ in a constant temperature agitator until evaporated completely to acquire the white solids. After dried completely and annealed at 600 ℃ for 2 h under N2 atmosphere, the puffed black material was washed with dilute hydrochloric acid and DI water. The prepared material dried overnight was denoted as BOC.

Synthesis of PDA-BOC: The desired amount of dopamine hydrochloride was added into 50 mL Tris buffer (pH 8.5). After vigorous stirring for 15 min, the solution was fully mixed with BOC and then heated to 60 ℃ for 24 h. The final mixture was washed and filtered with water/ethanol (volume ratio 1:1), and the sediment was dried under vacuum overnight at 60 ℃ to obtain the black product PDA-BOC.

The electrochemical performance of the as-prepared samples was tested in three-electrode system with platinum electrode, SCE (saturated calomel electrode) and 1 M NaCl as the counter, reference electrodes and electrolyte, respectively. The working electrode was fabricated by mixing active material, conductive graphite and polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) at a mass ratio of 80: 10: 10. Appropriate amount of ethanol was added and stirred to form a paste, then coated on pretreated nickel foam evenly. After overnight dried and pressed at 10 MPa, the working electrode was obtained. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) were measured under the potential window of −1 V ∼ 0 V. The specific capacitance of materials can be acquired by the following equation:
(1)
C m
=
I × Δ t
m × Δ V
where C m is the specific capacitance (F g−1); I represents discharge current (A); Δt is the discharge time (s); m is the mass of active substance (g); ΔV is the potential window (V).

Symmetrical supercapacitors (PDA-BOC//PDA-BOC) were prepared by two electrodes with the same mass of active substances and 6.0 M KOH as electrolyte. According to the GCD curve, energy density (E) and power density (P) can be calculated as follows:
(2)
E =
C m
× Δ
V 2
7.2
P = 3.6E/t(3)

where E (Wh kg−1) presents the energy density, C m (F/g) is gravimetric specific capacitance, ΔV (V) refers the applied potential range, P (kW kg−1) is power density, t (s) is the discharge time. CV, GCD and EIS tests of prepared electrodes were analyzed on electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, Shanghai Chenghua).

The desalination experiment was conducted using a batch-mode with a continuous recycling system. The symmetrical CDI device was composed of two pairs of 4 cm * 4 cm electrodes loaded with 200 mg ∼ 220 mg active substance. The complete system included a fluid reservoir, a peristaltic pump, a CDI unit-cell, a direct-current power supply and a conductivity meter to detect salt solutions. The specific operation parameters were as follows: the concentration of feed salt solution was divided into 200 mg/L, 500 mg/L and 1000 mg/L; the flow rate was 32 mL min−1; the total volume of salt solution was 80 mL; the applied potential differences were 1.0 V, 1.2 V, 1.4 V and 1.6 V; the solution temperature was 25 ℃. The relationship between electrical conductivity and salt solution concentration is shown in Fig. S1. The salt adsorption capacity (SAC, mg/g) of as-prepared electrode was determined by the equation below:
(4)
SAC
( mg
g
- 1
)
=
(
C 0
-
C t
)
V
tot
M
where C 0 and C t are the initial concentration and the one indexed to the adsorption time t (min), V tot (L) is the volume, and M (g) is the total mass of CDI electrode material.

The crystalline phases of the material were analyzed by the Bruker D8 super speed X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD, Cu Kα, λ = 1.5406 Å, 40 kV, 40 mA). The graphitization degree was measured by laser confocal Raman spectrometer (Raman, Renishaw, In Via, UK) with argon ion laser (k = 532 nm). The morphology and microstructure were analyzed by field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi, S-4800 II, Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, HT7800, with an acceleration voltage of 20 ∼ 120 kV). Surface analysis of samples was studied through Thermo Science ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using monochromatic AlKα radiation source (1486.6 eV). In the process of Ar ion etching, the angle between the Ar gun and the horizontal plane is 50°, and the base pressure of the analysis room is lower than 1.0 × 10-10 mbar. During the test, the pressure is lower than 2 × 10-7 mbar as the neutralization gun is opened.

In this work, carbon source and boron source were mixed evenly by means of dissolution-cocrystallization to ensure that the derived carbon materials perform uniformly in morphology and defects. Compared with the traditional alkali activation reacting on the carbon surface, the pore-forming mechanism in this work occurs from the inside out in the material phase, which is a more effective behavior. The SEM images (Fig. 2 D) illustrates the derived carbon material is fluffy carbon formed by stacking folded carbon cavities. This is due to the fact that during the carbonization process, the sodium carbonate, produced by the decomposition of the carbon source (trisodium citrate) at high temperatures, acts as both catalyst and template to generate a honeycomb-like structure. At the same time, the boron source (boric acid) releases large amounts of gas as a result of thermal pyrolyzing at high temperatures, further expanding the carbon material (Fig. 2E). Notably, the process of pore-forming during carbonization does not involve the conventional etching activation methods, making the preparation method safe and green. The crosslinked porous nanosheets to form a continuous conductive network and the large exposed specific surface area by sheet structure will facilitate the ions adsorption and electron transfer. In addition, the mutually supportive honeycomb-like structure also benefits the structural stability and durability of the material in the device. Fig. 2G displays bulk PDA-BOC with diameters greater than 10 μm, and the pore structure can be observed obviously all over the interior of the material. The Brunauer–Emmett Teller (BET) specific surface area and total pore volume of PDA-BOC is 1026.83 m2 g−1 and 2.54 cm3 g−1, respectively. Notably, the curve (Fig. S2A) shows a sharp increase at high pressure (P/P 0 greater than 0.9). Fig. S2B reveals a pore-size-distribution in the range of 2 nm −10 nm, demonstrating the mesoporous characteristics [33]. This is ascribed to the superiority of the co-crystallization method, which generates an inside-out activation in high temperature process [34]. Meanwhile, this also means the internal space of the carbon material will be utilized sufficiently during the ion adsorption process, so as to increase the energy density and power density of materials. However, the rich and disordered pore structure also increases the difficulty of electrolyte wetting (Fig. 2F). Hence, it is critical to enhance the material affinity for the electrolyte [35]. To sum up, a small amount of dopamine is adopted to in-situ polymerize at the interface of carbon materials to reinforce its affinity for aqueous electrolytes. As for improving the hydrophilic ability of materials, dopamine also plays a vital role in providing partially favorable pseudocapacitance, boosting the ion adsorption capacity.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns and Raman spectroscopy were used to explore the effects of dopamine polymerization on the crystalline structure (Fig. 2B) before and after polymerization process. Their XRD patterns show two characteristic peaks near 27° and 43°, indexed to the (002) and (101) lattice planes of amorphous carbon, respectively. The difference from the typical (002) plane value (24°) is from the abundant heteroatomic functional groups (O, B) and multiple functional groups in BOC [36]. Interestingly, these diffraction peaks barely change before and after dopamine polymerization, suggesting the mild reaction conditions. The ID/IG value for BOC remains unchanged after PDA functionalization in Raman analysis (Fig. 2C), in accordance with the XRD characterization.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) as a surface analysis method is used to determine the chemical properties and surface composition by measuring the binding energy of electrons. The XPS survey spectra contains four peaks at C 1s, N 1s, B 1s and O 1s, indicating PDA-BOC involves a variety of heteroatomic functional groups or defects. The atomic contents of C, O, N and B in PDA-BOC are 77.45%, 16.24%, 5.15% and 1.17%, respectively. The XPS spectra of BE is calibrated using the C–C component (284.8 eV). Oxygen atoms are mainly derived from oxygen-containing functional groups and defects formed by pyrolysis of carbon source (trisodium citrate dihydrate), centered at 531.1 eV, 532.6 eV and 533.9 eV, corresponding to carbonyl C = O (O-I), hydroxy C–OH/ether bonds C–O–C (O-II) and carboxyl COOH (O-III) in the O 1 s spectrum of PDA-BOC (Fig. 3 D). The FTIR spectrum (Fig S6) was measured to further verify the existence of COOH/C = O functional groups. The broad peak at 3435 cm−1 represents O–H stretching vibration, and the peaks near 1680-1 and 1031 cm−1 confirmed the presence of C = O stretching and –COO stretching [37]. Notably, O doping can improve the wettability of electrolyte and introduce more active sites, resulting in desirable reaction kinetics, which has been reported in previous literature [38–41]. To prove the wettability, we measured the hydrophilic characteristic of BOC and PDA-BOC provided by dynamic contact angle (Fig. S2). The dynamic contact angle of PDA-BOC is significantly lower than those of BOC. The high-resolution C 1 s spectra of PDA-BOC (Fig. 3B) can be deconvoluted into four peaks at 288.1 eV, 286.5 eV, 285.5 eV, and 284.8 eV corresponding to the existence of C═N, C-N/C-O, C-B and C–C, respectively. By comparison with BOC, the proportion of C-N/C-O is markedly enlarged (Fig. S3), owing to the PDA adsorption on the surface of the carbon sheet. The XPS results clearly demonstrate that the PDA are successfully coupled with carbon sheet and coated on the AC electrode surface. Besides, the B 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3E) can be assigned to four peaks B-N (191.3 eV), B-C (190.2 eV) and B-O (192.3 eV) in PDA-BOC. The presence of various types of B species confirms the existence of B-doped carbon lattice vacancies in PDA-BOC. According to the analysis above, it can be judged that BOC has O/B double doping defects. As for the N signal peak at the same position (Fig. S4), it is attributed to PDA coating [42].

On account of its pleasurable thermal stability, PDA-BOC experienced a weight loss of only 1.4 wt% (Fig. 3F) before heating to 600 °C in N2 atmosphere. The weight loss of PDA-BOC increasing to 5.3 wt% indicates the PDA degradation during heating. There are two degradation stages in weightlessness process, consistent with the two peaks at 363 °C and 480 °C in derivative thermogravimetric (DTG, Fig. S5) curve. These two degradation stages are due to sublimation/vaporization of organic matter and decomposition/recombination of hydrocarbon residues, respectively.

The considerable capacitive performance of PDA-BOC was tested in a three-electrode system. Fig. 4 A delivers the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curve data at a current rate of 5 mV s−1, significantly larger than BOC. It is known that the CV area measure is proportional to the specific capacitance, hence PDA-BOC has a larger specific capacitance than BOC. Except for offering additional pseudocapacitance, PDA also promotes the infiltration of electrolyte by its hydrophilicity, so as to improve the effective specific surface area. In the wide current rate range from 5 mV s−1 to 100 mV s−1, no obvious deformation can be observed in the CV curve (Fig. 4B), implying PDA-BOC has superior rate capability. From the GCD curve at the high current density of 10 A/g in Fig. 4C, its specific capacitance can still remain 61.8%.

of that at 0.5 A/g, which is superior to most of reported values [32,37,43,44]. Meanwhile, GCD curve under various current densities also maintains a high Coulombic efficiency close to 100% (Fig. 4D), revealing its highly reversible capacitive behavior. As shown in Fig. 4E, the capacitance retention of PDA-BOC underwent electrochemical cycling tests up to 500 cycles shows greater than 98% of the initial capacitance retention. No significant change is observed in its coulombic efficiency after 500 cycles, indicating its satisfied cycle stability and practical utility value.

The reaction kinetics were analyzed by Equ S1-3. As shown in Fig. 4E, the fitted value of b at −0.3 V, −0.1 V, 0.1 V, 0.3 V are 0.88, 0.89, 0.89, 0.87, suggesting the pseudocapacitance effect dominates the charge storage mechanism of material [45] PDA-BOC delivers a capacitive contribution of 79.86% at 20 mV s−1 and a columnar contribution map at different scan rate (Fig. 4G). As the scan rate increases (5 mV s−1 to 100 mV s−1), the capacitive contribution enhances from 72.78% to 92.27%, as exhibited in Fig. 4H. The prominent capacitive contribution is mainly due to the abundant active sites caused by B/O doping and dopamine interfacial modification.

In order to further verify the PDA-BOC adsorption of metal ions in different water bodies, four kinds of water quality in a complex environment were selected for testing, including Yangtze River (Fig. 6 A), Grand Canal (Fig. 6B), Slender West Lake (Fig. 6C) and rainwater (Fig. 6D). The metal ions reduction rate in water was measured by analyzing the inductively coupled plasma (ICP) values of the water before and after CDI treatment. The results revealed that ICP values of all kinds of metals in four kinds of water bodies were strikingly reduced after CDI treatment, suggesting the high removal efficiency. The PDA-BOC electrode with good desalination curves is suitable not only for the desalination of brackish water, but also for the removing of metal ions in complex situations. All in all, the abundant functional groups and defective structures on the PDA-BOC electrode surface opens a promising avenue for optimizing the ion adsorption and water resource protection by CDI technology.

With the continuous development of portable devices, the demand of flexible energy storage devices is boosting. In this work, PDA-BOC is utilized as electrode material for flexible solid-state capacitors and then assembled as a flexible symmetrical capacitor PDA-BOC//PDA-BOC. Fig. 7 A illustrates its component to form PDA-BOC//PDA-BOC, where carbon paper and KOH-PVA are used as a fluid collector loaded PDA-BOC active substance and electrolyte, respectively. The assembled device offers good flexibility and can be properly recovered after bending (Fig. 7B). CV curves under different bending degrees (0° ∼ 90°) remain almost unchanged (Fig. 7C), giving rise to its pleasant mechanical strength and flexibility. Meanwhile, the CV curve of PDA-BOC//PDA-BOC (Fig. 7C) displays a quasi-rectangle shape, revealing the existence of additional pseudocapacitance. As the scanning rate increases, the GCD curves (Fig. 7D) maintains a similar shape, which suggests its good rate capability. In fact, at a large current of 5.0 A/g (Fig. 7E), PDA-BOC//PDA-BOC can also remain 65% capacitance of that at the small current (0.3 A/g). Within the current densities ranging from 0.3 A/g to 5.0 A/g, the GCD curves of PDA-BOC//PDA-BOC (Fig. 7D) present symmetrical shapes. The Coulombic efficiency keeps stable around 100%, while PDA-BOC//PDA-BOC device experiences long cycles up to 10,000 times (Fig. 7G), confirming the admirable reversibility of charge–discharge reaction. The specific capacitance is calculated to be 86% of the initial capacitance after cycling, verifying good cycling durability. As illustrated in Ragone Plot (Fig. 7F), PDA-BOC//PDA-BOC supplies an optimal energy density of 12.5 Wh kg−1 and a maximum power density of 3.6 kW kg−1. Besides, Table S1 compares the electrochemical performance (energy density, power density and capacitance retention) of some reported materials. Above all, these desirable properties make PDA-BOC a brightening prospect in supercapacitors electrode active materials.

In this work, B, O-codoped honeycomb-like multi-chamber carbon abundant in COOH/C = O functional groups was prepared with low-cost organic reagents. By taking advantages of dopamine to regulate the functional group properties on carbon surface, the infiltration rate and accessible specific surface area of electrolyte are promoted efficiently. The synergistic effect of interfacial modification and confinement effect on the capacitance adsorption capacity was studied simultaneously. The multi-chamber structure of the carbon will act as a miniature reaction vessel to divide the limited space where ion adsorption occurs, not only enhancing the stability of the reaction environment, but also inhibiting the effective functional groups loss during the charge/discharge process. PDA functional groups grafting enormously improve the hydrophilicity and pseudocapacitance properties of the material interface. Therefore, PDA-BOC exhibits a remarkable electrosorption performance in electrochemical adsorption and energy storage conversion.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Key Research & Development Program (2021YFC2102205), Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (grant number KYCX21_3198), the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD), and Top-notch Academic Programs Project of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (TAPP, PPZY2015B112). The related measure and analysis instrument for this work was supported by the Testing Center of Yangzhou University.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2023.157995.

The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
